Refresh is split-brained: page.reload() re-pulls only the catalogue (status follows via the stdout chain), while closeDetail() re-pulls only status. StorePage's shared refresh calls extrasPage.reload() for this side. Three entry points, three different effects.
WifiTab.qml: ZERO CONFIG KEYS AND ZERO PERSISTENCE. This page writes nothing to any json/conf file. Its one setting (`Networking.wifiEnabled`) mutates live NetworkManager radio state, and everything else is live device/process state. There is no `defaults` object literal and no QtObject draft to diff against — a declarative settings-schema rewrite has nothing to bind to and no default to seed. Do not invent a config key for the Wi-Fi toggle; round-tripping it through a file would desync it from NM.

Truth is split across two backends. Quickshell.Networking supplies the device, the network list, signalStrength, connected, connect(), disconnect(). But security and known-profile state DO NOT EXIST on the service (comment, line 10-11) and are scraped from `nmcli -t` into `securityMap` / `knownProfiles`. Every row's "Secured"/"Open"/"Saved" label, the lock icon, and the whole tap-branch decision depend on shelled-out text parsing that can be stale for up to 1200ms after the list changes.

SECURITY CONSTRAINT, LOAD-BEARING: the password MUST go through stdin, never argv. `connProc.command = ["nmcli", "--ask", "dev", "wifi", "connect", ssid]` with the secret written in `onStarted`, because /proc/<pid>/cmdline is world-readable for the whole attempt (comment, line 103-104). Any rewrite that reaches for a simpler `nmcli ... password <pw>` form silently leaks credentials. `pendingPw` is also blanked the instant it is written.

MODEL CHURN VS. TYPING: an NM rescan hands back a fresh array, so the Repeater delegate — and the TextField inside it — is destroyed mid-typing (comment, line 43-44). The workaround is a page-level `pwDraft` plus `syncPwField()` (restores text, moves cursor to end, forceActiveFocus) fired from BOTH `onExpandedChanged` and `Component.onCompleted` via `Qt.callLater`. A generic declarative field will lose in-flight passwords and focus. This is the single most fragile mechanism on the page.

ORPHAN-PROFILE CLEANUP IS MANDATORY: a failed `nmcli dev wifi connect` leaves behind a connection profile named after the SSID. If it isn't deleted, `knownProfiles[ssid]` reads true forever and every subsequent tap takes the "known → connect()" branch and silently fails (comment, line 217-219, learned the hard way). The `attemptWasKnown` latch exists purely so cleanup never deletes a profile the user legitimately had before the attempt.

The scan spinner is a lie by design: `scanning` is driven by a fixed 10 000ms Timer, not by rescanProc exiting. The comment (line 117-119) is explicit that the scanner runs continuously anyway and this pulse is cosmetic. Making it "correct" (tie to process exit) is a behavior change.

`page.active` is hardcoded `true` and stays true for the object's whole life, because ConnectionsPage's Loader swaps `sourceComponent` on subtab change and destroys the page outright (comment, line 12-15 and 21-22). It is NOT a visibility flag to be wired up — it is a deliberate constant that the scannerEnabled Binding and the secRefresh timer both read. Re-plumbing it to real visibility changes the scanning lifecycle.

Networking is treated as optional at runtime: every access is guarded with `typeof Networking !== "undefined" && Networking` (lines 25, 27, 295). The page must degrade to an empty/off state rather than throw when the module is absent.
Method presence is also guarded — `typeof net.disconnect === "function"` / `typeof net.connect === "function"` (lines 87, 93). The network objects are not a trusted interface.

Single-expansion invariant: `expandedSsid` is one string, and the row-tap toggle (tapping an already-open row closes it, line 81-84) plus the X button plus a successful connect are three separate paths that clear it. `connectFailed` is a page-level singleton too, so the error text is scoped to whichever row happens to be expanded — it is not per-network.

`isSecured()` has three-way logic, not boolean: `undefined` (not yet scraped) is distinct from `""`/`"--"` (scraped, genuinely open). `activateNetwork` leans on that distinction via `var secKnown = securityMap[ssid] !== undefined` — an unscraped network falls through to the password row rather than being wrongly treated as open. Collapsing this to a bool breaks first-paint behavior.

The signal meter, the row, the password sheet, the X hit-target and the scrollbar are all hand-drawn primitives with hardcoded geometry (bar x/heights, 46px row, 44px sheet, 32px field, 21x16 meter). None map onto SettingSection/SettingField; they need to be carried over as bespoke components.
Nothing here previews-then-saves — every interaction is immediately destructive against real system state (radio off, disconnect, profile delete). There is no draft layer to stage a rewrite behind.
PluginSettingsForm.qml: The inventory is not static. Settings come from third-party plugin manifests at runtime, so no rewrite can be validated against a fixed key list. The only checkable contract is the type→control mapping, the coercion rules, and the field-shape keys ({key,type,label,default,options,min,max,step,decimals,group,placeholder}). Dropping a schema key silently disables that feature for every plugin using it, with no compile-time or runtime error.

`default: return cText` (line 97) is a deliberate catch-all for text + unknown types. Rewriting the switch as exhaustive (or throwing/skipping on unknown type) silently breaks any plugin declaring a type this build doesn't know — the forward-compat escape hatch.

Falsy-zero trap: min/max/step/decimals all use `field.X !== undefined ? field.X : <fallback>`. Rewriting with `??` is safe; rewriting with `||` silently corrupts the common cases `min: 0` (→0, coincidentally fine), `step: 0`, and especially `decimals: 0` (→2), which also disables the `decimals === 0` integer-rounding branch on write. Ints would start persisting as fractions.

Slider `modified` fires live per knob movement (SliderRow.qml:6,62), so every drag tick calls _set → changed → a fresh `ryoku-plugins-place` subprocess. No debounce, no commit-on-release. A drag spawns a burst of processes racing to rewrite plugins.json, and setSetting reassigns `settingsProc.running = true` while a prior run may be in flight. Preserve the liveness or add debounce deliberately — do not silently change to commit-on-release.

Type-loose read / strict write asymmetry. Toggle accepts boolean true OR the string "true" but writes a real boolean. Dropdown compares `String(_val(field))` but writes the raw option key. So JSON-typed values drift on first edit: a plugin storing "true" gets true, a numeric option key may round-trip as a different type. Existing plugins.json values depend on this leniency on read.

Group headers are positional, not a real grouping pass: `startsGroup` only compares against `schema[index-1]`. A schema with the same group name in non-adjacent positions emits the header twice, and fields are never reordered/bucketed. A rewrite that groups properly (e.g. bucketing by group name) changes rendering for existing manifests.

Image writes a file:// URL (`"" + imgDlg.selectedFile`), while display strips to basename. Plugins consuming the value receive a URL, not a path. Normalizing to a plain path in a rewrite would break every plugin that already parses the URL form, and would change bytes already on disk.

JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) round-trip on `values` silently drops undefined, functions, and Infinity/NaN→null, and will throw on cyclic input. It is doing double duty as deep-clone and as the binding-invalidation trigger (_set reassigns _local wholesale). Replacing it with a shallow copy or in-place mutation breaks change propagation, not just cloning.

No validation, no error surface, no dirty/save state. Malformed schema entries (missing key, options absent on a choice, non-numeric default on a slider) fail soft and silently — Dropdown gets [], Number(undefined) → NaN. Every edit is immediately persisted; there is no Save/Cancel/Revert to model.

Both cText and cImage are bespoke inline `component` blocks with no shared counterpart in the hub control set (there is a SettingField.qml and ImagePicker.qml in the same directory, deliberately NOT used here). Swapping them for the shared controls during a rewrite changes geometry (fixed 220x30), commit timing (onEditingFinished), and the focus/hover animation behavior.
KeybindsPage.qml: The default tab (Shortcuts) is NOT a settings surface and cannot be made declarative. Its whole content is regex-scraped from ~/.config/hypr/modules/binds.lua by Go (parseBinds, keybinds.go:55): categories come from `-- Section` line comments, descriptions from a trailing comment or else heuristic dispatcher/exec sniffing (describeDispatcher/describeExec), and the workspace `for` loop collapses into a synthetic "1…0" range key. There is no static list to check a rewrite against — the legend's contents change with binds.lua.

Key display names are a hardcoded Go prettyKey map (keybinds.go:155-180): SUPER->Super, SHIFT->Shift, ALT->Alt, CTRL/CONTROL->Ctrl, Return->Enter, comma->",", grave->backtick, Left/Right/Up/Down->arrow glyphs, mouse:272->LMB, mouse:273->RMB, mouse_up/mouse_down->"Scroll ↑/↓", XF86Audio{RaiseVolume,LowerVolume,Mute,Play,Next,Prev}->Vol +/Vol −/Mute/Play/Next/Prev. Unmapped tokens pass through raw. This mapping is invisible to QML and must not be reinvented there.

HyprStore holds the ENTIRE Hyprland override document (appearance, input, cursor, env, windowRules, layerRules, appOverrides, autostart, keybinds, anim, plugins). This page instantiates its OWN `HyprStore { id: store }`, so `store.dirty` and the Save button are scoped to the whole doc, not to keybinds — pressing Save here writes every draft field. Each page gets a separate store instance, so cross-page drafts do not share state.

Lists deliberately do NOT live-preview while scalars do (HyprStore.qml:195-200 vs edit()/queuePreview()). Every setting on this page is live:false, which is a property of the store's list path, not of the controls. A rewrite that routes keybinds through edit() instead of editList() would start firing `ryoku-hub hypr preview` on every keystroke.

Conflict detection is imperative JS with no declarative equivalent: normKeys() lowercases, trims and SORTS modifier tokens before comparing; shippedKeys is rebuilt from the injected `categories`; rowConflict() returns "" | "shipped" | "duplicate"; conflictCount relies on the `void store.rev;` reactivity hack to re-evaluate because store.keybinds mutations don't otherwise notify. Conflicts are computed readouts, never persisted.
Conflicts are advisory only — nothing blocks Save. A row that shadows a shipped bind or duplicates another custom row saves happily and just loses at runtime.

Silent data loss in the backend: genKeybind() (hypr.go:1160) returns "" — emitting nothing to settings.lua — when keys is blank, when action is exec/"" with a blank value, or when action is anything outside the 4 known keys. The row survives in hypr.json and in the UI but never becomes a bind, with no user feedback.

Row `value` is conditionally rendered via `needsValue`, which treats action===undefined as exec. That undefined branch exists to tolerate legacy rows persisted without an action key — a strict typed model would drop them.

Rows are positional: identity is the array index, there is no id. patch/remove/add rebuild the array with slice + Object.assign + splice. Any reorder, keyed delegate, or model-diffing rewrite changes semantics.

Both text fields commit on onEditingFinished, not on textChanged. Edits are lost if the row is deleted or the tab is switched while a field still has focus — and switching tabs destroys the Loader subtree outright.
Leaving the page with unsaved edits drops the live preview (documented in the HyprStore header comment), so tab/page navigation has side effects on compositor state.
The keys field is free text with zero validation — no key-capture widget, no check against Hyprland key names. Users must hand-type Hyprland syntax; the intro text is the only guidance.

Hardcoded pixel arithmetic in the row layout: `width: parent.width - 200 - 230 - delBtn.width - 40` (KeybindsEditor.qml:225) hard-couples the command field to the 200px keys field, the 230px Dropdown and the delete button. Row height is a fixed 56, legend rows a fixed 44.
Segmented has a fixed segW of 86 and positions its pill by `4 + indexOfKey(current) * segW`; labels wider than 86px overflow, and indexOfKey silently returns 0 for an unknown key.

`categories` is a `property var` injected from Hub.qml and consumed by both tabs for different purposes (legend rendering vs conflict detection). The page neither owns nor validates it; before `ryoku-hub keybinds` returns it is [], so conflict detection is silently inert on first paint.

The fade NumberAnimation targets `loader.item`, an object replaced on every tab switch, and sets opacity imperatively in onLoaded — a binding-based rewrite must preserve the 0->1 restart or the new tab renders invisible.

KeybindGroup carries a `tagged` property used only by SearchResults.qml (global sidebar search renders the same legend groups outside this page). Changing KeybindGroup's contract to suit a KeybindsPage rewrite would break search results.

store.revert() does not just restore the draft — it shells out to `ryoku-hub hypr restore` because hyprctl eval cannot push some keywords back to a default or re-derive wallpaper-derived border colours. The reload is required, not incidental.

The page has no SettingSection anywhere and no PageHeader — the header comes from Hub.qml's sectionDefs. A rewrite onto standard SettingSection/SettingField scaffolding would be a visual redesign, not a like-for-like port.
BluetoothTab.qml: THIS PAGE HAS NO CONFIG BACKING AT ALL. It imports only Theme from "Singletons" — no Config singleton, no FileView, no defaults object literal, no QtObject draft, no save/apply path. Nothing on it is written to any json/conf file. A declarative settings model has literally nothing to bind to here; every value is live BlueZ D-Bus state or a shelled-out command. Treat it as a host/device page with one incidental ToggleRow, not as a settings page.

The adapter ToggleRow is NOT a plain bool write. setAdapterEnabled(v) branches on page.blocked (rfkill): when blocked and turning on, it fires `rfkill unblock bluetooth` and RETURNS without touching adapter.enabled; the process's onExited then sets enabled = true. A generic ToggleRow bound directly to adapter.enabled will silently no-op on a blocked radio — this is the exact bug the indirection exists to fix.

The toggle is conditionally absent, not disabled: visible: page.hasAdapter. When bluetoothd is gone the switch vanishes and a `pkexec systemctl enable --now bluetooth.service` repair button takes the whole page over. A declarative row that only supports enabled/disabled cannot express "a switch that can't act shouldn't look live".

ToggleRow is used with label: "" and width: 56, hard-overriding its implicitWidth of 320. It renders as a bare header-band switch, not a labelled setting row. Any rewrite that routes it through SettingSection will re-introduce the label column and the 320px width and break the header layout.

The Scan control is stateful and self-cancelling: it flips adapter.discovering AND arms/disarms a 25s auto-stop Timer, and Component.onDestruction force-stops discovery on page teardown. It is a toggle with a lifecycle and a timeout — not expressible as a setting row, and not safely expressible as a plain action button either.

Device rows are polymorphic single-tap: the same gesture means disconnect (connected), connect (paired), or run the full bluetoothctl pair→trust→connect pipeline (unpaired). One control, three verbs, chosen at tap time from live state.

Pairing is a shell pipeline with a per-device transient overlay: pairProc.command is rebuilt for each device, guarded by pairProc.running (only one pair at a time, globally), and drives pairingAddress/failedAddress which the delegates match on by address string. The "Pairing failed" line self-erases after 4s. None of this is declarative state.

Hard-coded magic numbers with no config key and no UI to change them: 25000ms scan auto-stop, 4000ms pair-failure line, 6000ms service-error line, `timeout 30` on both bluetoothctl pair and connect. A rewrite must carry them verbatim or behaviour drifts.

devicesSorted implements a bespoke 4-tier rank sort (connected → paired → named → nameless MAC) with a localeCompare tie-break, run on every devices change. The ordering is load-bearing UX ("a scan shouldn't churn the useful rows around"), not incidental.

batteryLevel() does unit-sniffing: it accepts BOTH 0..1 fractions and 0..100 percentages from BlueZ (b <= 1 → b * 100), and overloads -1 as "unknown" alongside <= 0. Any typed/declarative model that pins this to a single unit will mis-render one of the two backends.

metaFor() dedupes BluetoothDeviceState.toString(d.state).toLowerCase() against the literal strings "connected"/"paired" already pushed — a string-identity coupling to a Quickshell enum's stringification that will break silently if the enum names change.

Defensive `typeof Bluetooth !== "undefined"`, `typeof BluetoothAdapterState !== "undefined"`, `typeof BluetoothDeviceState !== "undefined"` and `typeof d.connect === "function"` guards throughout: the whole Quickshell.Bluetooth module is treated as possibly-absent at runtime. Every derived property has a null-adapter fallback.

Ember is hard-coded as rgba(242/255, 86/255, 35/255, α) at five different alphas (0.14, 0.16, 0.12, 0.30, 0.32, 0.40) rather than going through a Theme token, so it will NOT follow a palette/wallust reskin the way Theme.ember does.

svcProc raises a polkit prompt (pkexec → hyprpolkitagent) — an out-of-process, user-cancellable, possibly-hanging modal that the page only observes via exitCode. Non-zero covers both "user cancelled" and "systemctl failed" with the same generic message.

Not the default subtab: ConnectionsPage.qml opens with sub: "wifi" and swaps BluetoothTab in via a Loader sourceComponent. The page is destroyed on every subtab switch, which is what makes Component.onDestruction the scan-stop hook — a rewrite that keeps all three subtabs alive (e.g. StackLayout) will never fire it and will leave the radio scanning.
KeybindsEditor.qml: Not a settings form at all — there is not a single SettingSection, ToggleRow, SliderRow, NumberField, ChoiceRow, ColorField, SettingField or Segmented in this file. It is a variable-length list editor. A declarative 'group → labelled row' rewrite has no schema to hang onto: the 'settings' are three fields repeated per user-created row, and the only fixed key on disk is the `keybinds` array.

No stable row identity. add/remove/patch all work by array index on a sliced copy (a[i] = Object.assign({}, a[i])), and the Repeater model IS store.keybinds — so every single patch reassigns the array and rebuilds all delegates. Any rewrite using keyed/persistent delegates or an ObjectModel changes focus/edit behaviour; any rewrite that keeps this pattern inherits the focus churn.

Text commits only on onEditingFinished (Enter or focus loss). A user who types a combo and clicks Save without leaving the field loses that keystroke run — the draft never saw it, so Save writes the stale value. This is a real edge in the current code, and a naive rewrite to onTextChanged would change dirty/preview semantics.

Conflict detection is imperative and depends on a `void store.rev` read to force re-evaluation (conflictCount). normKeys/customCount/rowConflict/shippedKeys are hand-rolled JS over the model, O(n²) across rows, and shippedKeys is derived from an externally parsed file (binds.lua) that is not part of the store. None of this survives as a declarative property without keeping the rev-bump hack or restructuring the store.

Conflicts are advisory only: Save stays enabled and writes conflicting binds anyway. The status text hijacks the dirty indicator (a conflict message replaces 'Unsaved shortcuts'), so a rewrite that separates validation from dirty state will change what the bar says.

Silent lossy round-trip: rows with blank keys, exec rows with blank value, and rows with an unrecognised action are all persisted to hypr.json but silently emit nothing from Go genKeybind() — so they survive reload, still occupy a row, and are simply inert. There is no validation UI for this.

The `value` field is conditionally hidden by action (needsValue) but never cleared, so a row switched exec→close→exec keeps its old command. Value is only meaningful for exec; a declarative schema would want a dependent/nullable field.
The enum lives in two places that must stay in sync: page.actionOpts here (4 options) and the switch in genKeybind() in ryoku/hub/backend/hypr.go. Adding a dispatcher requires both.

Whole-doc store coupling: this page instantiates its own HyprStore, which loads and saves the ENTIRE Hyprland doc. store.dirty is a JSON.stringify comparison of the full snapshot against committed (so key ordering matters), and Save writes appearance/input/cursor/rules/plugins back too — if another page saved in between, this page's Save clobbers with its loaded copy. Clear all + Save is therefore not a keybinds-scoped write.

Lists deliberately do NOT live-preview (editList skips queuePreview) while the same store previews scalars live — so this page's Save/Revert semantics differ from its sibling settings pages by design. Revert additionally shells out to `ryoku-hub hypr restore` (a full regen + reload), not just a state rollback.

Layout is arithmetic, not layouts: the command field width is `parent.width - 200 - 230 - delBtn.width - 40`, and delBtn is anchored to the row rather than living in the Row. Any control-size change silently breaks the fit.

Dropdown is used in a mode the component was not really shaped for: label:"" inside a row, with fieldWidth 150 inside width 230, and a Popup that positions relative to the field. Inside a clipped Flickable this is a popup-in-a-scroller.
'Clear all' is destructive, always enabled, unconfirmed, and there is no resetKeybinds() in HyprStore — the only recovery is Revert (which needs the change to still be uncommitted).

The editor is destroyed on tab switch by the parent Loader, taking the unsaved draft with it and dropping it with no prompt (HyprStore's own comment acknowledges 'leaving a page with unsaved edits drops the preview').
AnimationsPage.qml: THE ANIMATIONS TABLE IS RUNTIME-DERIVED, NOT A FIXED SETTING LIST. Rows come from `hyprctl animations -j` filtered by `a.overridden && a.name.indexOf("__") !== 0`. The 15 leaves inventoried here (borderangle, windowsIn, fadeOut, fade, border, workspaces, global, windowsOut, layers, fadeIn, specialWorkspace, windows, fadeLayersIn, fadeLayersOut, windowsMove) are THIS machine's current snapshot out of the 35 leaves Hyprland reports. The set shifts with the shipped config, the Hyprland version, and loaded plugins (e.g. hyprfocusIn/hyprfocusOut and dynamic-cursors-magnification only exist because those plugins are loaded). Any rewrite that hardcodes this list is wrong.

animProc has `running: true` with no re-trigger and no reload hook. The table is a construction-time snapshot: after Save (which regenerates settings.lua and reloads), newly-overridden leaves do NOT appear until the page is rebuilt. Encoding the list declaratively would freeze a value that is already stale by design.

`overridden` is self-referential: saving an override makes a leaf overridden, so the row set can grow after a reload but never shrinks back within a session. A leaf you reset to default can vanish from the table on next visit, taking its row with it.

itemOf() is a THREE-LEVEL FALLBACK (store.animItems -> liveAnims -> {enabled:true, speed:1, bezier:"", style:""}). Rows display live compositor values while nothing is persisted. There is no 'default' in the settings.lua sense for a per-leaf item — the Go default is literally `Anim{Items: [], Curves: []}`. A declarative model with per-key defaults cannot express 'shows the compositor's value until touched'.

upsertItem() SNAPSHOTS ALL FOUR FIELDS. Nudging speed on a row also freezes that row's enabled/bezier/style at their current live values into hypr.json. From then on the row stops tracking the compositor — a later change to the shipped decoration.lua silently stops applying to any leaf the user ever touched. This write-amplification is invisible in the UI.

The style Dropdown's option keys do not cover the live values. specialWorkspace is live at 'slidefadevert 20%' but the options are bare ('slidevert', 'slidefadevert'); layers is live at 'popin 90%' which IS in the layers set, while the windows set offers 'popin 80%'. Hyprland styles carry an optional percentage argument that this enum cannot represent — choosing any option silently drops it.

AnimRow abuses Dropdown's placeholder: `label: ""` + `placeholder: row.style` means an unmatched style renders the RAW live string as if it were a selection, and an empty style renders a BLANK field instead of 'Default' (even though '' is a real option labelled 'Default'). Fixing this to a well-behaved ChoiceRow changes what several rows display.

'Reset to defaults' on this page is a PARTIAL reset: it only does editAnim("animItems", []) + editAnim("animCurves", []). It does not touch the GLOBAL Animations toggle, does not touch any FOCUS FLASH/hyprfocus setting, and never consults store.defaults — unlike resetAppearance()/resetInput() which read store.defaults per key.

CROSS-PAGE COUPLING, hyprfocus: AppearancePage's 'Reset to defaults' calls store.resetAppearance(), which calls resetPlugins() and returns hyprfocus (owned by THIS page) to Enabled:false, Mode:flash, Opacity:0.8, Bounce:0.95, Slide:20. Resetting Appearance silently wipes this page's FOCUS FLASH group.
CROSS-PAGE COUPLING, animations key: `appearance.animations` is the same key bound by AppearancePage.qml:463. Two pages own one toggle; whichever saves last wins the whole document.

MIXED PREVIEW SEMANTICS WITHIN ONE PAGE. GLOBAL (store.edit), CURVES and ANIMATIONS (store.editAnim) all preview live via hyprctl eval; FOCUS FLASH (store.editPlugin) does NOT — plugins load on reload, so it lands only on Save. A uniform declarative 'live' flag per page cannot express this, and the page compensates with prose ('Applies on Save.').

HyprStore is instantiated PER PAGE and holds the ENTIRE override document (appearance + input + cursor + env + windowRules + layerRules + appOverrides + autostart + keybinds + anim + plugins). save() serialises the whole snapshot, so saving from Animations rewrites every other page's settings from this page's copy. Two hub pages open on the same doc is a lost-update race.

`dirty` is computed by JSON.stringify(snapshot()) !== JSON.stringify(committed) — key-order- and float-formatting-sensitive whole-document string comparison, re-evaluated on every `rev` bump. It is not per-setting dirty tracking, so a declarative per-field model has nothing to hang off.

Curve identity is the NAME STRING and there is no rename UI. Names are auto-generated ('custom', 'custom1', 'custom2', ... via a linear probe against curveNames()). upsertCurve() matches by name, so a name collision silently overwrites.

A user curve can SHADOW a built-in: curveOf() checks store.animCurves before liveCurves, and curveNames() de-dupes live-first. Selecting 'ryokuBloom' and dragging a handle writes a user curve named ryokuBloom that overrides the shipped one — which is why the button reads 'Reset' (selectedHasOverride) rather than 'Delete' (selectedIsCustom) for built-ins. Two different meanings behind one button.

resetCurve() has a hidden side effect: `if (page.selectedIsCustom && page.liveCurves.length > 0) page.selectedCurve = page.liveCurves[0].name` — deleting a custom curve silently re-selects an unrelated curve. Note selectedIsCustom is evaluated AFTER the store edit, so it still reads true against liveCurves.

Asymmetric, non-obvious ranges on the bezier handles: X clamped [0,1] (Hyprland needs monotonic-in-time) but Y clamped [-1,2] to allow bounce overshoot. The canvas geometry (pad 18, yMin -1, yMax 2) IS the validation — px/py/ux/uy are the only thing preventing an out-of-range value that breaks the config on reload.

selectedCurve is initialised by a side effect inside the Process stdout handler (`if (page.selectedCurve === "" && liveCurves.length > 0) selectedCurve = liveCurves[0].name`) — an async, ordering-dependent default that no declarative default can express.

The Go generator prunes on write: genAnimItem omits `bezier`/`style` entirely when empty and skips items with a blank leaf; genAnimBlock skips curves with a blank/whitespace name. So '' is both a legal enum option ('Default') and a value that disappears from settings.lua. Round-tripping through the file is lossy in a way the UI does not show.

Two config files, one truth: ~/.config/ryoku/hypr.json is the editable store; ~/.config/hypr/settings.lua is GENERATED from it (diffed against defaultOverrides()) and must not be hand-edited. Only divergence from the shipped baseline is ever written, so 'what is in the file' and 'what the UI shows' are deliberately different.

Speed stepper granularity is enforced by float-safe arithmetic, not a step property: Math.round((speed ± 0.1) * 10) / 10 with Math.max(0.1)/Math.min(10) clamps. A naive SliderRow/NumberField with step:0.1 would reintroduce float drift.

borderangle is listed as a tunable row but is also written by the Appearance 'animated border' path (genBorderAngle emits `hl.animation({ leaf = "borderangle", ... style = "loop" })` with borderAngleSpeed). Same for windowsMove/windowsIn/windowsOut via wobbly-windows and windowStyle. Editing these rows here fights generated lines from another page.

Revert cannot be done by state restore alone — store.revert() must shell out to `ryoku-hub hypr restore` because hyprctl eval cannot push some keywords back to a default and cannot re-derive wallpaper-driven border colours. The comment notes the page must still be alive for the process to run; Hub.qml duplicates this call in go() for exactly that reason.
PluginPlacementEditor.qml: No shared setting components at all — zero ToggleRow / SliderRow / NumberField / ChoiceRow / Dropdown / ColorField / SettingField / Segmented, and no SettingSection wrapper. Both real settings are expressed as direct manipulation (a spatial drag canvas + a bespoke pill row). A declarative rewrite cannot express `align` as a row without discarding the interaction model.

`align` has no discrete control whatsoever. Its only input is a physical drag with a midpoint snap (line 148). It can be flattened to a 2-value Segmented, but the reserved-band affordance is the ONLY thing that explains to a user why centre is missing — drop the canvas and the constraint becomes arbitrary.

Coupled positional write: edge/align/hoverW/hoverH are not independent keys. Every edit shells out `ryoku-plugins-place <id> framePopout <edge> <align> <hoverW> <hoverH>` — one command, four positional args. Per-key declarative bindings MUST re-send all four; omitting args 3/4 makes the bash writer substitute 320/16 (ryoku-plugins-place line 84) and silently reset the user's hover zone.

Defaults are duplicated across three files and DIVERGE. edge: "right" here (line 25) vs "top" in the runtime (PluginPopouts.qml:92). align/hoverW/hoverH: "start"/320/16 in QML (lines 26,28,29), again in bash (ryoku-plugins-place line 84), again in the runtime (PluginPopouts.qml:93,127,128 — where hoverW/hoverH instead fall back to 0). No single source of truth to point a `def` at.

No `defaults` object literal and no QtObject draft property exist in the file. Defaults are inline ternary fallbacks on the READ path only, so a default is observable as a display value but is never written to disk — a plugin can sit with no framePopout block at all and the editor still renders a plausible-looking state.

No two-way binding and no writeback. `place` is a plain untyped `var` handed down from discover.sh JSON; the write goes out-of-band through a Process in the parent. The chip's dragged position is local-only until the Registry file watch re-lists and pushes a new `place` — if that refresh is missed, the chip visually snaps back to the stale value while disk holds the new one.

Per-plugin, not global. The component instantiates once per installed plugin card, so the config key is `<pluginId>.framePopout.*` — a path parameterised by pluginId, not a fixed key a static settings schema can name.

Truthiness reads make 0 unrepresentable: hoverW()/hoverH() use `? :` on the value itself (lines 28-29), so a legitimately stored 0 collapses back to 320/16. A zero-width hover zone cannot be expressed. Same pattern at PluginPopouts.qml:127-128 (there collapsing to 0 instead — opposite direction).

Centre is never a legal value. A rewrite offering start/center/end would write a value the shell cannot honour: the centre of every edge is pre-reserved by the shell (top=island, left=mixer, right=power menu, bottom=clear for symmetry).

Geometry is derived from `edge` through four-way ternary chains for x/y/width/height on both the reserved band (lines 75-83) and the body chip (lines 110-123), plus the `vertical` computed property (line 27). The vertical/horizontal asymmetry is load-bearing and non-obvious: band thickness 64 vs 60, chip 64x96 vs 96x60, shared margin m=10 declared separately in each (lines 73, 117).

The Canvas dashed border is imperative (onPaint + manual requestPaint on width/height change) — it will not survive a purely declarative port without a Shape/border substitute, and it repaints on every edge change since the band resizes.

No draft/apply/cancel model. Edits commit on tap and on drag-release, write live to plugins.json, and the running shell retunes immediately (per the file header). There is no staging buffer, no Save, and no revert — a settings framework that assumes a draft-then-commit lifecycle has nowhere to put this.

Scope trap: only the framePopout host is editable here. desktopWidget placement (x/y/scale/locked — all real, all written by the same ryoku-plugins-place script, lines 69-82) is deliberately out of scope and owned by wallpaper drag + PluginWidgetMenu.qml. A rewrite that "completes" this editor by adding desktop-widget fields would duplicate an existing surface.

The `host` setting ("Show as": framePopout | desktopWidget) sits immediately above this component in PluginsPage.qml:393-436 and gates its visibility, but is NOT owned by this file. It is easy to absorb by mistake during a rewrite — or to lose, since it is likewise a bespoke Repeater pill row filtered to hosts the manifest declares.
HotspotTab.qml: No Ryoku config file at all. Every value on this page persists in NetworkManager's own store (/etc/NetworkManager/system-connections/RyokuHotspot.nmconnection) and is written by shelling out to nmcli. There is no JSON/conf key, no defaults object, no QtObject draft — a declarative settings framework that writes ryoku config will silently lose the entire page unless it can model an external-command-backed source.

State is bidirectional and can change out from under the UI: hsName/hsPw/hsActive are READ FROM the system on mount (Component.onCompleted) and after every mutation. nmcli, nm-applet, or another machine touching the profile desyncs the UI until the next refresh. A one-way "config → disk" model cannot express this.

Create-or-modify branch: the first apply runs `nmcli connection add` (and is the ONLY path that sets ifname, mode=ap, ipv4.method=shared, autoconnect=no); every later apply runs `nmcli connection modify` and touches only ssid/key-mgmt/psk. Consequence: hsIface is baked in at first-use and never rewritten — swapping Wi-Fi hardware leaves a stale ifname with no UI to fix it.

Secret-in-argv discipline is load-bearing: SSID/password/iface ride as positional args ($1/$2/$3) and are explicitly NEVER spliced into the shell string (see the comment at line 38). A rewrite that string-formats these values into a command is a shell-injection bug, not a style regression.
Password read-back needs `nmcli -s` (secrets). If the read fails or returns nothing, hsReadProc silently leaves hsPw as-is — the page shows a stale or empty password with no error.

Auto-generation is a cross-setting side effect: flipping the Hotspot toggle ON with no valid password silently REWRITES the Password setting (generatePw). One control mutating another's value has no clean declarative expression.

Silent drops: commitHotspotEdit() discards an empty name and discards a password under 8 chars with no feedback — the editor closes and the old value stays. The inline warning only appears mid-typing, never at commit. Looks like a successful save; isn't.
Editing a value while the hotspot is live re-applies and bounces the AP (`if (page.hsActive) page.applyHotspot()`), dropping connected clients. A "setting write" here is a service restart.

Single shared edit-mode state: page.hsEdit (a field-name string) plus page.hsDraft (one draft slot) means exactly one row can be in edit mode at a time, and the draft is page-level, not row-level. Rewriting to per-row bound TextFields changes commit/cancel semantics.

Asymmetric commit/cancel: focus-loss COMMITS (onActiveFocusChanged → commitHotspotEdit) but Esc CANCELS (Keys.onEscapePressed clears hsEdit/hsDraft). A generic SettingField that commits on any focus change, or cancels on blur, will invert real behavior.

CredRow is a bespoke `component` declared inline inside the SettingSection — it uses none of the hub's standard rows (no SettingField, ChoiceRow, Dropdown). Its idiom (tap-the-value-to-edit, masked bullets padded to min 8, per-row reveal, mono+ember secret styling, ElideLeft) is not reproducible by any existing shared control.

Boolean-ish toggle is really an action pair: `checked: page.hsActive` is a system readout, not a stored preference. There is no "hotspot enabled" value anywhere — the toggle issues `connection up` / `connection down` and waits for the probe to confirm. Modeling it as a persisted bool will resurrect the hotspot at odd times (note autoconnect=no is deliberate).

Async state race: refreshHotspot() fires hsStateProc and hsReadProc independently with no ordering or completion join, and hsBusy is cleared in onExited before the refresh lands — the toggle and status text can flicker through a wrong state.

Zero failure handling: no Process exit code is ever inspected. A failed add/modify/up just clears hsBusy and refreshes; the toggle springs back with no error surface. Any rewrite that adds error reporting is a behavior change, and any rewrite that preserves it inherits a silent-failure page.

hsIface derives from `Networking.devices.values.find(d => d.type === DeviceType.Wifi)` behind a `typeof Networking !== "undefined"` guard, with a hardcoded "wlan0" fallback — it is a live binding on a service that may be absent, and it feeds both the status readout and the profile creation.
HyprStore.qml: The prompt's premise is wrong and that is itself the top hazard: this is a store, not a page. A rewrite that treats it as a settings page and 'ports the controls' will produce nothing; a rewrite that treats it as empty will delete the key+default contract for 9 pages. Every one of the 94 keys above must survive even though none is user-visible in this file.

`dirty` is a JSON.stringify deep-compare of snapshot() vs committed — it is KEY-ORDER DEPENDENT. snapshot() builds objects by iterating appearanceKeys/inputKeys/cursorKeys in array order, so both sides happen to agree. A rewrite that uses real QtObject properties, Object.keys(), or reorders the key arrays will silently break dirty detection (permanently dirty, or never dirty).

`rev` is a manual invalidation counter with a `void store.rev;` read inside the `dirty` binding — a deliberate hack because QML does not track mutations inside `var` arrays/objects. Any declarative rewrite must keep an equivalent invalidation trigger or dirty freezes.

cursorProp() name mangling: the disk keys are cursor.theme/size/inactiveTimeout/hideOnKeyPress but the store properties are cursorTheme/cursorSize/cursorInactiveTimeout/cursorHideOnKeyPress — prefixed and capitalised only to avoid clashing with the appearance namespace. The property name is NOT the config key for exactly these four. Trivially lost in a mechanical rewrite.

save() writes `committed` optimistically BEFORE saveProc reports anything — no exit-code check, no onExited, no error surface. A failed save leaves the UI showing clean. Same for previewProc/restoreProc: all three are bare `Process { }` with no stdout/stderr/exit handling.

`plugins` is a nested plain-JS `var` object mutated by JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(...)) deep-copy-and-reassign (editPlugin). It has no per-key properties, no type checking, and no binding granularity — pages reach in with store.plugins.hyprbars.height. Expressing these 27 keys as real declarative settings changes the whole read path in AppearancePage.

TWO SOURCES OF TRUTH for defaults: the QML initial property values (lines 22-107) duplicate defaultOverrides() in ryoku/hub/backend/hypr.go by hand. I verified they currently match exactly. They are only a pre-adopt() placeholder — getProc overwrites them at startup — but they drift silently, and the Go comment ('keep these in step with the base') admits it. A third copy exists in the shipped decoration.lua/input.lua/keyboard.lua modules.

No min/max/step/unit anywhere in the store — every range lives page-side on the SliderRow/NumberField (e.g. hyprbars height from:12 to:48 at AppearancePage.qml:344). The store neither clamps nor validates, so a declarative rewrite has no range data to hoist and must go read all nine pages to recover it.

Enum option lists are NOT here either. The store holds bare strings/ints. I could verify only layout [dwindle|master|scrolling] and dynamicCursors.mode [rotate|tilt|stretch]. windowStyle, accelProfile, followMouse, hyprglass.preset and hyprfocus.mode have option lists that exist ONLY on AppearancePage/InputPage — those five enums are contract holes in this file and must be recovered from the page inventories.

Preview asymmetry resists a uniform declarative model: appearance/input/cursor scalars preview live; env/windowRules/layerRules/appOverrides/autostart/keybinds do NOT (they land on Save+reload, because eval cannot undo a removal); plugins do NOT (a plugin only loads on reload); but animItems/animCurves DO preview despite being lists. Four different write semantics behind one `dirty` flag.

Cross-domain side effect: resetAppearance() also resets cursor AND all plugins (including hyprfocus), justified only by a comment that the plugin toggles happen to be rendered on Appearance's tabs. The reset scope is coupled to UI layout, not to the data model — moving a plugin toggle to another page silently changes what Reset does.

Defaults arrive from a DIFFERENT process than the document (defProc vs getProc), unordered and unawaited. resetAppearance()/resetInput()/resetPlugins() are silent no-ops if defProc has not returned yet — every reset is guarded by `if (... !== undefined)` / `if (store.defaults.plugins)` and just does nothing otherwise. No loading state distinguishes this from a working reset.
`ready` gates `dirty` but nothing gates the mutators: edit() before getProc returns would mutate the placeholder draft and preview it, then adopt() would silently overwrite the user's edit.

The lists (env/windowRules/layerRules/appOverrides/autostart/keybinds/anim.items/anim.curves) are opaque arrays of records whose schema is defined in Go structs, not in QML. They are not scalar settings and cannot be expressed as declarative rows without importing the record shapes from the backend.

previewNow() re-serializes the entire document (all 94 keys + every list) into an argv string every 80ms during a slider drag, and shells a process per tick. Argv length grows with the user's rule/bind count — a big keybinds list makes every preview more expensive.

hyprglass.tint uses a bare 8-digit AARRGGBB string with no '#' while glowColor/activeBorder/inactiveBorder use '#rrggbb'. A unified ColorField that normalises to one format will corrupt one of the two encodings.
imgborders.sizes/insets pack four numbers into a single CSV string ('8,8,8,8'). They look like one setting and behave like four; a naive split into four NumberFields changes the disk format.

activeBorder/inactiveBorder defaults are not reliably what is live — revert()'s comment says wallpaper-derived border colours cannot be re-derived by eval. The stored '#e0563b'/'#313a4d' may be overwritten by the wallust/wallpaper path outside this store's knowledge.

Divergence-only persistence (Go side): only values differing from defaultOverrides() are written to settings.lua, so untouched settings fall through to the base module and pick up future base changes. Several defaults are deliberately NEVER written (windowStyle 'pop' is not pinned; animatedBorder off is silent — hypr_test.go:319/350/373). A rewrite that starts writing every key pins users to today's baseline forever and breaks those tests.

hyprscrolling is the one plugin with no `enabled` key — it is gated by appearance.layout === 'scrolling'. A rewrite that regularises 'every plugin section has an enable' will invent a key the backend does not read.

Leaving a page with unsaved edits drops the preview (per the header comment) but the throttle Timer and previewPending live on the store, which outlives the page — the documented behaviour depends on the page's lifetime, not on any code in this file.
